coredata.dc:title: Development of a highly sensitive electrochemiluminescence sophoridine sensor using Ru(bpy)3 2+ integrated carbon quantum dots – polyvinyl alcohol composite film
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zhimin
Zhang, Xing
Cui, Lin
Wang, Kang
Zhan, Haijun
coredata.dc:description: A facile electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing platform for the sensitive analysis of sophoridine was constructed using Ru(bpy)3 2+/carbon quantum dots – polyvinyl alcohol composite film as modifier. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which have the ability to enhance the ECL intensity were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method. As Ru(bpy)3 2+ was entrapped in the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), it was beneficial to immobilize Ru(bpy)3 2+ efficiently on the GCE surface, and therefore a stable ECL signal could be obtained. In addition, the ECL response on the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/PVA composite system was greatly enhanced by sophoridine. Single-factor was applied to optimize the measurement conditions. The fabricated sophoridine sensor exhibited a well-proportional correlation between ECL intensity and the logarithm of sophoridine concentration in the range of 1.0×10−10–1.0×10−5 M, and the detection limit was 5.0×10−11 M (S/N=3). Moreover, the detection of sophoridine in blood serum was successfully accomplished and a desirable result by using the ECL sensing system was achieved, which indicated that the fabricated ECL sensor has potential value in sophoridine monitoring in practical samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Sensor
Carbon quantum dots
Sophoridine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of the developed ECL sensor
ECL behavior of sophoridine at different electrodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the fabrication process of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE ECL sensor.
TEM images of the prepared CQDs.
SEM images of (A) PVA/GCE and (B) CQDs-PVA/GCE.

(A) CVs of CQDs-PVA/GCE (a), Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA/GCE (b) and Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE (c) in 0.1M PBS (pH 8.5) at a scan rate of 80mVs−1. (B) CVs of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE in PBS (pH 8.5) at different scan rates (from inner to outer: 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.12, 0.14, 0.16, 0.18, 0.2Vs−1). Inset is the plot of peak current vs the square root of scan rate.

The ECL intensity–potential curve of CQDs-PVA/GCE (a), Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA/GCE (b) and/Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE (c) in 0.1M PBS (pH 8.5) containing 10mM sophoridine. ECL signals were obtained by sweeping the potential between 0.2V and 1.25V at a scan rate of 80mVs−1.

(A) Effect of (A) pH, (B) scan rate, (C) concentration of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and (D) VCQD: VPVA on the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE sensor in PBS (pH 8.5) containing 10mM sophoridine. Scan rate: 80mVs−1.

(A) ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE sensor for different concentration of sophoridine (a→f: 0, 1.0×10−9，1.0×10−8，1.0×10−7，1.0×10−6，1.0×10−5 M). (B) The ECL intensity of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE sensor versus the negative logarithm of sophoridine concentration.
Comparison of sensitivities of this work with other methods for the determination of sophoridine.
Application of the proposed ECL method to the determination of sophoridine in spiked serum samples (n=3).
Application of the HPLC method to the determination of sophoridine in spiked serum samples (n=3).

head.ce:title: Development of a highly sensitive electrochemiluminescence sophoridine sensor using Ru(bpy)3 2+ integrated carbon quantum dots – polyvinyl alcohol composite film

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: 100 North Zhongyuan Road, Zhengzhou 450001, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing platform for the sensitive analysis of sophoridine was constructed using Ru(bpy)3 2+/carbon quantum dots – polyvinyl alcohol composite film as modifier. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which have the ability to enhance the ECL intensity were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method. As Ru(bpy)3 2+ was entrapped in the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), it was beneficial to immobilize Ru(bpy)3 2+ efficiently on the GCE surface, and therefore a stable ECL signal could be obtained. In addition, the ECL response on the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs/PVA composite system was greatly enhanced by sophoridine. Single-factor was applied to optimize the measurement conditions. The fabricated sophoridine sensor exhibited a well-proportional correlation between ECL intensity and the logarithm of sophoridine concentration in the range of 1.0×10−10–1.0×10−5 M, and the detection limit was 5.0×10−11 M (S/N=3). Moreover, the detection of sophoridine in blood serum was successfully accomplished and a desirable result by using the ECL sensing system was achieved, which indicated that the fabricated ECL sensor has potential value in sophoridine monitoring in practical samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Sophoridine is a white alkaloid crystal extracted from traditional medicine herb Sophora alopecuroides L. [1]. Some reports have shown that sophoridine has extensive pharmacological effects and preventive effects on myocardial infarction [2–4]. Importantly, it also shows the potential therapeutic efficacy on colorectal carcinoma [5,6]. Therefore, the quantitative determination of sophoridine is very critical in clinical analysis. Until now, different analytical techniques have been reported for measuring sophoridine concentration including high performance liquid chromatography [7], gas chromatography–mass spectrometer [8], nonaqueous capillary electrophoresis [9], electrochemical method [10,11] and electrogenerated chemiluminescence [12–14]. However, some of these methods are usually time-consuming, expensive or need a complicated sample preparation before measurement. In addition, considering the importance for practical use, these operation systems lack the ability to achieve a real-time analysis since they cannot be miniaturized. Accordingly, it is urgent to exploit an accurate and sensitive method for rapid analysis of sophoridine.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a light-emitting process of species electrochemically produced at matrix via electron transfer, which possesses the advantages of electrochemistry and chemiluminescent [15]. Recently, ECL has received special attention owing to its wide application in food safety [16,17], pharmaceutical analysis [18], environmental monitor [19,20], immunoassays [21] and bioanalysis [22–24]. Among a series of ECL luminophores, ruthenium (II) tris(bipyridine) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) has been extensively used for its excellent sensitivity, high efficiency and favorable electrochemical stability [25,26]. Compared with liquid -state ECL applications, solid-state ECL sensors have prominent advantages including saving the cost of expensive Ru(bpy)3 2+, reducing environmental pollution and simplifying experimental process [27]. Furthermore, effective immobilization method of Ru(bpy)3 2+ could make ECL reagent closer to the electrode surface, resulting in further improvement of the ECL signal of Ru(bpy)3 2+. Therefore, many efforts are being made in order to increase the immobilized amount of luminophore on the electrode surface to construct highly-sensitive and cost-saving solid-state ECL sensors.

In recent years, various methods including sol–gel technology [28], self-assembly [29] and Langmuir–Blodgett technique [30] have been extensively applied to immobilize Ru(bpy)3 2+. However, the reported ECL techniques have some shortcomings, including poor stability owing to the leakage of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and low sensitivity owing to the immobilized amount of Ru(bpy)3 2+ on the modified electrode surface. In order to eliminate the potential drawbacks, different new nanomaterials with unique properties for developing Ru(bpy)3 2+-based sensors are being found, for example semiconductor nanomaterials [21], metal nanoparticles [31,32] and carbon-based nanostructures [25]. Among these nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of photoluminescence carbonaceous material, has received broad interests owing to their unique characteristics including superior water solubility, high biocompatibility and low toxicity [33]. Besides, CQDs are prepared by hydrothermal technique which is regarded as an environmentally friendly and cost-efficient method. Taking the unique properties of CQDs into consideration, we believe that CQDs may be ideal candidates as carriers for fabricating sensitive ECL solid-state sensor. On the other hand, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a man-made polymer with good biocompatibility, superior film-forming characteristics and exceptionally mechanical properties, which offers compatible microenvironment for the immobilization of biomolecules [34]. Combining the advantageous properties of CQDs and PVA, in the present work, we fabricated a ECL sensor with high stability and sensitivity for accurate sophoridine detection using Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA composite modified electrode. The ECL signal of the developed sensor is significantly enhanced with the increasing of sophoridine concentration. Additionally, the as-proposed ECL sensor displays higher sensitivity and wider linear range, suggesting that the developed composite film is a proper matrix for Ru(bpy)3 2+ immobilization which can be applied for the construction of a highly sensitive solid-state ECL sensor. More importantly, the developed sensing system provides a new way to expand the applications of CQDs in sensors.

Tris (2,2-bipyridyl) dichlororuthenium hexahydrate [Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O] (>98%) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with an average molecule weight 50,000 and 99% hydrolysis degree was purchased from Beijing Huamei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). First patent flour was obtained from Jinyuan Flour Co., Ltd. (Zhengzhou, China). Potassium periodate (KIO4) was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sophoridine was purchased from National Institutes for Food and Drug Control (Beijing, China). Standard stock solution of sophoridine (10mM) was prepared with water and stored in refrigerator. 0.1M phosphate buffered solutions (PBS) with different pH values were prepared with Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. All the other reagents were of analytical grade and used without extra treatment. Doubly distilled water was used throughout this work. Human blood samples were offered by the First Affiliated Hospital of Zhengzhou University and treated prior to use.

CHI 660E electrochemistry workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) was employed for cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements. The ECL signal was collected using MPI-A electrochemliuminescence analyzer (Xi’an Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Xi’An, China) with the voltage of 800V supplied by photo-multiplier tube. A three-electrode electrochemical system was used with modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, d=3mm) as working electrode, platinum wire as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode as reference. JEM 1200EX transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were carried out for monitoring the surface morphologies of CQDs and modified electrodes, respectively. Fluorescence spectra were performed on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Varian, American). UV absorption spectra were obtained using a U-4100 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier Transform-Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on Shimadzu Prestige-21. Electrical conductivity data were obtained by a ZEM-3 measurement system (Ulvac Riko, Inc.).

CQDs were prepared according to the reported method [35]. Briefly, 4.0g of flour, 1.0g KIO4, 8mL HCl and 20mL of doubly distilled water were mixed and dispersed uniformly under ultrasonication for 20min. Next, the mixture was placed into a stainless-steel autoclave (100mL) and allowed for reaction at 200°C for 4h. The prepared carbon source was cleaned with NaOH, water and ethanol, respectively. Subsequently, 0.4g of carbon source and 120mL of H2O2-HAc oxidant (V(H2O2): V(HAc)=2:1) were added into a round-bottom flask, and reacted for 10h at 110°C. Finally, yellow CQDs solution was collected. The prepared CQDs solution was dialyzed for 36h and then evaporated. Light yellow CDs powder could be obtained by vacuum drying.
5g of PVA was added into 100mL purified water, and the mixture was heated at a fixed temperature of 80°C for 4h in order to obtain uniform polymer solution (5%, Wt/V).

Before electrode modification, each GCE was polished with 0.3, 0.05μm alumina powder sequentially and then ultrasonically cleaned with distilled water. Afterward, GCE was dried under ambient condition. A 5μL aliquot of Ru(bpy)3 2+ solution (2.0mM) was first dropped onto GCE surface and dried in air. Subsequently, CQDs soution (12mgmL−1) was mixed with PVA (5%, Wt/V), and 5μL of the mixture were coated onto the Ru(bpy)3 2+/GCE surface. Lastly, the obtained Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE electrode was cleaned with PBS solution. The prepared ECL sensors were kept in refrigerator when not in use. The preparation process of modified Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The collected serum samples were mixed with equal volume of ethanol and centrifuged at 12000rpm for 10min. Then the supernatant was adjusted to pH 8.5 with 0.1M PBS. Meanwhile, spiked serum samples were prepared by adding different sophoridine standard solutions into the serum to obtain final concentrations of 0.1μM, 0.5μM, 0.8μM, 2.0μM and 6.0μM. The samples were transferred into the electrochemical cell for the measurement of ECL intensity using the as-prepared Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE sensor.

FT-IR measurement was employed to confirm the functional groups of as-synthesized CQDs (Fig. S1A). The broad peak at about 3443cm−1 indicates the presence of OH bond. The two obvious absorption peaks located at 1722 and 1217cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration mode of CO and CO, respectively. Therefore, the above characteristic absorption bands demonstrate the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups and carboxylic acid. Additionally, the existing characteristic peaks at 2932 and 1634cm−1 reveal the presence of CH2 and CC on the surface of CQDs [35].

From UV–vis absorption spectrum, it can be seen that CQDs exhibited a wide absorption peak around 220nm (Fig. S1B) [36]. The yellow CQDs solution emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV light. Furthermore, the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained around 450nm when excitation wavelength was fixed at 380nm (Fig. S1C).

TEM was performed to characterize the morphology of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , the CQDs were well dispersed sphere particles and showed a diameter distribution in the range of 8–10nm. SEM was adopted to investigate the surface morphologies of the electrodes. Seen from the SEM image (shown in Fig. S2), the Ru(bpy)3 2+ modified surface was relatively uniform, and a number of Ru(bpy)3 2+ were clearly exhibited on the electrode surface. The surface morphology of PVA/GCE was displayed in Fig. 2 (A), a well-distributed PVA network was observed on the GCE surface, which could stop immobilized molecules from leaking. Fig. 2(B) presented the SEM image of CQDs-PVA/GCE. It was clearly seen that CQDs was densely encapsulated into the PVA matrix, with the obvious bright dots being CQDs molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of the developed ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammogram (CV) technique was a useful way to investigate the electrochemical (EC) behavior of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE system. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, no clear EC response was observed from the CQDs-PVA/GCE sensor (curve a). On the contrary, the CVs of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA/GCE (curve b) and Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE (curve c) gave a pair of typical redox peaks owing to the electron transfer between Ru(bpy)3 3+ and Ru(bpy)3 2+, which was related to the EC behavior of Ru(bpy)3 2+. Additionally, the peak current intensity of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE (curve c) was ca. 1.5-fold higher than that of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA/GCE (curve b). The conductivity of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA composite film was measured to be 204Sm−1 according to the four-point probe technique in our work. After the addition of CQDs, the conductivity of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA film was increased to 3.42×103 Sm−1. The observed enhancement in electrochemical behavior was probably attributed to the fact that CQDs had the ability to enhance the conductivity of the composite film and facilitate the electron exchange between the modified electrode and the buffer solution.

Fig. 3B shows CVs of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE obtained in 0.1M PBS (pH 8.5) with various scan rates from 20 to 200mVs−1. The oxidation peak current (Ipa) and reduction peak current (Ipc) of Ru(bpy)3 2+ displayed a well-proportional correlation to the square root of scan rate (v 1/2) (Fig. 3B, inset), the regression equation was Ipa =131.908 v 1/2 +0.331 (R2 =0.9967) and Ipc =−33.467 v 1/2 +5.455 (R2 =0.9994), respectively. The results illustrated that the oxidation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ at Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE was confined by diffusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behavior of sophoridine at different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
A sensitive method was depicted in the present work for enhancing the ECL signal by introducing CQDs into the GCE electrode. The ECL signals of different modified electrodes were recorded in PBS (pH 8.5) in the presence of 10mM sophoridine as coreactant. As suggested in Fig. 4 , no ECL emission was detected by the CQDs-PVA/GCE sensor (curve a). In contrast, obvious ECL signal was observed on Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA/GCE (curve b) and Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE (curve c). Obviously, these ECL signals came from the immobilized Ru(bpy)3 2+ on the modified GCE surface, which produced excited-states and the corresponding ECL signal could be observed. The mechanism of Ru(bpy)3 2+- sophoridine is outlined below:
Ru(bpy)3 2+ −e− →Ru(bpy)3 3+
sophoridine−e− →[sophoridine ]+
[sophoridine ]+ →sophoridine +H+
Ru(bpy)3 3+ +sophoridine →Ru(bpy)3 2+* +sophoridine
Ru(bpy)3 2+* →Ru(bpy)3 2+ +hv

The ECL intensity was 10594.0 a.u. on the Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA/GCE electrode, and 17355.0 a.u on the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE electrode with a 63.8% increase in ECL intensity. Also, Fig. 4 shows that the ECL onset potential on Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE (Fig. 4c) is about 1.035V and lower than that observed on Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA/GCE (Fig. 4b, 1.125V). As compared Ru(bpy)3 2+/PVA/GCE, the noticeable enhancement in the ECL signal on Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE together with a negative shift (about 90mV) in its ECL onset potential was ascribed to the outstanding electroconductivity of CQDs, which could tremendously promote the electronic exchange between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and GCE. Accordingly, higher ECL intensity and lower onset potential could be obtained.

The effect of various parameters namely pH, scan rate, Ru(bpy)3 2+ loadings and volume ratio of VCQDs: VPVA on ECL behaviors of Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE towards sophoridine determination was discussed. As indicated in Fig. 5 A, the correlation between ECL intensity and the pH of PBS was studied. It can be seen that the ECL response increased with the pH value changing from 6.5 to 8.5, but gradually decreased thereafter. This observation was in agreement with the ECL mechanism of Ru(bpy)3 2+-tetracyclines system [37]. The ECL signal enhanced from 6.5 to 8.5, suggesting the fact that the deprotonation of sophoridine was needed in reaction system. However, when pH value was higher than 8.5, a competitive reaction between OH− ions and Ru(bpy)3 2+ became more dominant, which led to the consumption of Ru(bpy)3 2+ [38]. As a result, an optimized pH value was determined to be 8.5.

It is also necessary to consider the effect of scan rate on the ECL intensity. As depicted in Fig. 5B, the ECL signal increased accordingly with the increase of the scan rate, and achieved the peak value at 0.08Vs−1. This may be ascribed to the fact that the ECL response was affected by the formation and diffusion of the intermediate on the modified electrode [39]. A lower scan rate may reduce the formation of the intermediate, which resulted in a lower ECL intensity. However, at higher scan rate, shorter reaction time led to smaller transmission of the reaction, which also resulted in a lower ECL signal. Accordingly, 0.08Vs−1 was selected for subsequent experiments.

Furthermore, the relationship between the ECL intensity and Ru(bpy)3 2+ concentration was investigated over the range of 0.1–4.0mM (Fig. 5C). The ECL signal showed obvious increase with the increasing concentration of Ru(bpy)3 2+ within 2.0mM, however, when the concentration of Ru(bpy)3 2+ was higher than 2.0mM, there was no obvious increase. Therefore, 2.0mM Ru(bpy)3 2+ was chosen as the optimal value.

Moreover, because the composite was obtained by mixing CQDs (12mgmL−1) and PVA (5%, Wt/V), we investigated the effect of the volume ratio of composite (VCQDs: VPVA). As indicated in Fig. 5D, the ECL signal increased accordingly while the ratio of composite was increased, and reached the maxima at 1:8. However, when the ratio was more than 1:8, the ECL intensity began to drop. It could be explained that excessive PVA shall increase the thickness of the composite film, thus hinder the diffusion of sophoridine into the solid matrix. Therefore, the volume ratio of 1:8 was selected for the modification of the ECL sensor.

Under the optimal conditions, the standard curve for sophoridine detection was achieved based on the Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE composite modified electrode. The ECL intensity-time curve showed that a stable ECL response could be obtained within 10s. The ECL responses to the concentrations of sophoridine were displayed in Fig. 6 A. The ECL responses of the developed solid-state sensor increased with the increase of sophoridine concentration. Importantly, the ECL signal intensity exhibited a well-linear correlation to the logarithmic of sophoridine concentration ranging from 1.0×10−10–1.0×10−5 M, and the linear regression equation was I ECL =2.53×104+ 1.69×103logc with a correlation coefficient of 0.994 (Fig. 6B). The detection limit was 5.0×10−11 M (S/N=3). The result indicated that the proposed sensor was sensitive in the detection of sophoridine. The comparison between the performance of proposed ECL sensor and other reported methods in the literature was summarized in Table 1 . Compared with liquid chromatography [7], gas chromatography–mass spectrometer [8], capillary electrophoresis [9] and electroanalytical techniques [10,11], the developed ECL sensor was more sensitive for detecting sophoridine in the low concentration regime. Moreover, even same ECL techniques, the detection window of the fabricated sensor was much wider than those reported earlier (2.8×10−8–4.4×10−7 M [13] and 2.5×10−8–2.0×10−6 M [14] for the ECL methods). Meanwhile, a detection limit of 5.0×10−11 M was lower than the 1.2×10−10 M for solution-phase ECL sensor [12]. The improved performance showed by the proposed method may be due to two reasons. One reason is that a large amount of Ru(bpy)3 2+ encapsulated in the CQDs-PVA matrix could improve the ECL response signal, leading to an increase in sensitivity. The other one is the introduction of CQDs which could work as an electron bridge allowing more Ru(bpy)3 2+ to participate in the reaction, thus further improved the ECL intensity.

We also evaluated the repeatability, reproducibility and stability of the ECL sensor. For eight successive measurements, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.8% with the same ECL sensor was achieved. The reproducibility of six sensors, which were fabricated in the same way, is found to yield an RSD of 4.8%. The long term stability of the constructed ECL sensor was investigated by recording the ECL intensity every two days while storing the sensor in refrigerator between use. The ECL intensity maintained 87% of original response after one month. As discussed above, the proposed ECL fabrication strategy with CQDs-PVA nanocomposite shows a promising fabrication reproducibility and stability.

It is well known that the ECL sensor is extremely sensitive to the co-existing substance. In our experiments, interference studies were performed by selecting some interferents that probably existed in human serum. The tolerant concentration limit for the interferent was detected, it was found that the interferent brought an ECL response change of ±5%. Based on the experimental results, the acceptable concentration ratios for interference species were over 1000-fold for Mg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Al3+, SO4 2−, Cl−, glucose, citric acid, oxalic acid or 50-fold for uric acid and ascorbic acid, indicating that the developed ECL solid sensor displayed advantageous selectivity in detecting sophoridine in the presence of some common ions and organic compounds. However, the matrine and oxymatrine interfered with the detection because of their similar structure as sophoridine.

The possibility of the developed ECL sensing system in physiological sample was assessed. The Ru(bpy)3 2+/CQDs-PVA/GCE sensor was employed to analyze sophoridine in spiked human serum sample using standard addition method. The serum sample preparation containing 1.00×10−7, 5.00×10−7, 8.00×10−7, 2.00×10−6, 4.00×10−6 M sophoridine was described in the experimental part (Section 2.5) and the results were concluded in Table 2 . The recoveries of sophoridine ranged from 93.5% to 107.8%. Meanwhile, the spiked human serum sample was determined by traditional HPLC method, the obtained results were summarized in Table 3 . It was found that the recovery results of sophoridine using ECL sensor were comparable with those obtained with HPLC. This means that the fabricated ECL sensor supplied a potential method for determining sophoridine in practical samples.

In summary, a novel Ru(bpy)3 2+-based ECL sensor was proposed for sophoridine analysis by using CQDs-PVA nanocomposite. It is the first time that CQDs have been used in the sensing of sophoridine. On the one hand, the introduction of CQDs in the nanocomposite could improve the conductivity and increase the electron transmission rate. In addition, CQDs could function as electron tunnel, which allows more Ru(bpy)3 2+ to be involved in the reaction system, leading to an enhanced ECL signal. Furthermore, the encapsulation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ into the PVA matrix efficiently reduced the leakage of Ru(bpy)3 2+, thus enhanced the stability of the developed sensor. Finally, the ECL sensor was adopted for sophoridine analysis in human serum and the results were satisfactory, indicating strong potentials for practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21275039) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Henan Provincial Colleges and Universities in Henan University of Technology (No. 2016RCJH03).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.03.115.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Studies on the alkaloids of Sophora alopecuroides
Anti-arrhythmic effects of sophoridine and oxysophoridine
Antiviral effects of sophoridine against coxsackievirus B3 and its pharmacokineticsin rats
Pharmacological action of alkaloids from Sophora alopecuroidesl
In vitro anti-tumour activities of quinolizidine alkaloids derived from Sophora Flavescens Ait
Synthesis, structure-activity relationship and biological evaluation of anticancer activity for novel N-substituted sophoridinic acid derivatives
A sensitive and specific HPLC-MS method for the determination of sophoridine, sophocarpine and matrine in rabbit plasma
Simultaneous determination of four alkaloids in Gan-Yan-Ling injection by GC–MS
Determination of quinolizidine alkaloidsin traditional Chinese herbal drugs by nonaqueous capillary electrophoresis
A simple, rapid and green method based on pulsed potentiostatic electrodeposition of reduced graphene oxide on glass carbon electrode for sensitive voltammetric detection of sophoridine
Electrochemical behavior of sophoridine at a new amperometric sensor based on L-Theanine modified electrode and its sensitive determination
Determination of sophoridine and related lupin alkaloids using tris(2,2′-bipyridine) ruthenium electrogenerated chemiluminescence
Analysis of quinolizidine alkaloids in Sophora flavescens Ait. by capillary electrophoresis with tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II)-based electrochemiluminescence detection
A novel solid-state electrochemiluminescence detector for capillary electrophoresis based on tris(2,2′-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) immobilized in Nafion/PTC-NH2 composite film
A novel mesoporous molecularsieves-based electrochemilumenescence sensor for sensitive detection of azithromycin
A novel solid-state electrochemiluminescence sensor for melamine with Ru(bpy)3 2+/mesoporous silica nanospheres/Nafion composite modified electrode
Recent development of electrochemiluminescence sensors for food analysis
Sensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor based on ordered mesoporous carbon composite film for dopamine
A reusable potassium ion biosensor based on electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer
Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer based on Ru(bpy)3 2+)-doped silica nanoparticles and its application in turn-on detection of ozone

Sensitive ECL immunosensor for detection of retinol-binding protein based on double-assisted signal amplification strategy of multiwalled carbon nanotubes and Ru(bpy)3 2+ doped mesoporous silica nanospheres
A novel quantum dot nanocluster as versatile probe for electrochemiluminescence and electrochemical assays of DNA and cancer cells
Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles labeling for a sandwich-type electrochemiluminescence immunosensor
Ultra sensitive apurinic/apyrimidinic endonuclease 1 immunosensing based on self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence of a Ru(II) complex
One-step immobilization of tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II) via vapor-surface sol-gel deposition towards solid-state electrochemiluminescence detection
Solid-state electrochemiluminescence sensor through the electrodeposition of Ru(bpy)3 2+/AuNPs/chitosan composite film onto electrode
Electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+ loaded in Nafion Langmuir–Blodgett films: role of the interfacial ultrathin film
An ECL sensor for dopamine using reduced graphene oxide/multiwall carbon nanotubes/gold nanoparticles
Sandwich-format electrochemiluminescence assays for tumor marker based on PAMAM dendrimer-L-cysteine-hollow gold nanosphere nanocomposites
Photoluminescent carbon dots as biocompatible nanoprobes for targeting cancer cells in vitro
Preparation of PVA membrane for immobilization of GOD for glucose biosensor
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, characterization, and assessment of cytocompatibility
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
A novel electrochemiluminescence tetracyclines sensor based on a Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles/Nafion film modified electrode
A single calibration graph for the direct determination of ascorbic and dehydroascorbic acids by electrogenerated lumi-nescence based on Ru(bpy)3 2+ in aqueous solution
Generation of chemiluminescence upon reac-tion of aliphatic amines with tris(2,2′-bipyridine)ruthenium(III)

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhimin Liu is a professor in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology. Her main research interests involve chemical sensors, biosensors and electroanalytical chemistry.
Xing Zhang is currently pursuing his Master’s degree in Henan University of Technology. His research interest is mainly focused on electrochemical biosensors.
Lin Cui is currently a MS candidate in Henan University of Technology. Her research activity is mainly focused on electrochemical biosensors.
Kang Wang is currently a MS candidate in Henan University of Technology. His current research work is concentrated on electrochemical biosensors.
Haijun Zhan, Associate Professor of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology. His research interest is mainly focused on electrochemical biosensors.
